---
title: "Correcting for gaseous absorption"
author: "François Steinmetz"
format:
    html:
        toc: true
        number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
execute:
    echo: false
    warning: false
---

# High spectral resolution line-by-line dataset

Gas optical depths per layer are obtained for each considered gas from the CKDMIP high-resolution line-by-line database [@hogan2022] via the [`gatiab`](https://github.com/hygeos/gatiab) interface, where the atmospheric profiles are provided by AFGL [@anderson1986]. By default the AFGL-US profile is used. These profiles are then integrated to obtain, for each gas, the total column optical depth at a high spectral resolution, $T_1(\lambda)$, for unit air mass and a nominal gas quantity $U = U_0$ (see @tbl-nominal-gas).



: Nominal gas concentrations $U_0$ for each atmospheric gas. {#tbl-nominal-gas}

| Gas |             $U_0$ |
| :-- | ----------------: |
| CH₄ | 9.46 × 10⁻⁴ g/cm² |
| CO₂ |       0.519 g/cm² |
| H₂O |        1.42 g/cm² |
| N₂  |         782 g/cm² |
| N₂O | 4.83 × 10⁻⁴ g/cm² |
| O₂  |         239 g/cm² |
| O₃  |        345 Dobson |


The numerical values of $T_1(\lambda)$ are stored in the file `gaseous_absorption_model_gatiab_ckdmip_v1.nc` for each gas.

```{python}
#| label: fig-all-gases
#| fig-cap: "High-resolution transmission $T_1$ for all gases (up to 800 nm)."
from tests.test_gaseous import plot_all_gases
plot_all_gases()
```

# Integrated transmission model

The monochromatic transmission coefficient for an arbitrary air mass $M$ and gas content $U$ is obtained as $T_1(\lambda)^x$, where $T_1(\lambda)$ is given for each gas at $x=1$, with $x = M \cdot U/U_0$ (air mass × gas content / nominal gas content).

For a given sensor band with SRF $S(\lambda)$, the integrated transmission is defined as:

$$\bar{T}(x) = \frac{\int S(\lambda) T_1(\lambda)^x \, d\lambda}{\int S(\lambda) \, d\lambda}$$ {#eq-integ1}

And its derivative is:

$$\bar{T}'(x) = \frac{\int S(\lambda) T_1(\lambda)^x \ln T_1(\lambda) \, d\lambda}{\int S(\lambda) \, d\lambda}$$ {#eq-integ2}

We model the integrated transmission as:

$$\bar{T}(x) = T_\text{eq}^{(x^n)}$$ {#eq-model}

Then, the derivative of $\bar{T}(x)$ at $x=x_0$ is:

$${\bar{T}'}(x_0) = n T_\text{eq}^{x_0^n} \ln T_\text{eq} \cdot x_0^{n-1}$$

Using $\bar{T}(x_0) = T_\text{eq}^{x_0^n}$ and $\ln \bar{T}(x_0) = x_0^n \ln T_\text{eq}$, this expression simplifies:

$${\bar{T}'}(x_0) = n \cdot \frac{\bar{T}(x_0)\,\ln \bar{T}(x_0)}{x_0}$$

Then $n$ can be solved by:

$$n = \frac{{\bar{T}'}(x_0) \cdot x_0}{\bar{T}(x_0) \cdot \ln \bar{T}(x_0)}$$ {#eq-n-solution}

The values of $\bar{T}(x_0)$ and $\bar{T'}(x_0)$ are calculated using equations [-@eq-integ1] and [-@eq-integ2].

For $T_\text{eq}$, and having solved for $n$, @eq-model gives:

$$T_\text{eq} = \bar{T}(x_0)^{\,x_0^{-n}}$$ {#eq-Teq-solution}
